title: Getting a CPH number before your first livestock
subtitle: What a County Parish Holding number is, who needs one, and how to apply before any livestock arrives.
excerpt: A County Parish Holding number is a legal requirement before you keep any cattle, sheep, goats, pigs, or deer in England. It also applies to poultry if you keep more than 50 birds. The number identifies your land in the national livestock tracing system and is required for movement licences, veterinary medicine records, and government disease surveillance. You cannot legally receive livestock without one.
sourceNotes:
Animal & Plant Health Agency (APHA), CPH registration guidance. https://www.gov.uk/guidance/register-land-you-use-to-keep-livestock
Rural Payments Agency, CPH and holding registration. https://www.gov.uk/guidance/rural-payments-agency

glossaryTerms.definition: County Parish Holding number: a nine-digit reference in the format 00/000/0000 that uniquely identifies a parcel of land in the livestock management system. The first two digits are the county code, the next three the parish, and the final four digits are unique to the holding.

Animal and Plant Health Agency: the government agency responsible for safeguarding animal and plant health in Great Britain. APHA holds the national livestock register and administers movement licences, disease testing, and farm inspections.

content.text: is assigned to the land, not the keeper. If you are renting or grazing someone else's land, the landowner may already have a CPH. Check before applying: a single parcel of land should have only one CPH number, though multiple keepers can hold their own herd or flock numbers registered to the same CPH.

You need a CPH before keeping any cattle, sheep, goats, pigs, or deer. In England, poultry keepers of more than 50 birds must also register. Scotland and Wales have similar requirements through their respective government agencies.
administers registration in England. Contact details and the online application portal are at gov.uk.

Register through the Rural Payments Agency (RPA) online portal or by calling the RPA helpline. You will need to describe the land (address, approximate area, and the species you intend to keep) and provide proof of the land's location if requested. Processing typically takes five to ten working days. Start the application before you source any livestock: the number must be in place before animals arrive.

Once you have your CPH, you register a separate herd or flock number for each species you keep. Cattle have a herd mark (a six-digit code used on ear tag numbers); sheep and goats have a flock number (eight digits); pigs have a herd number. Each species is registered separately with APHA. These numbers appear on movement licences, ear tags, and veterinary medicine records. Apply for them at the same time as the CPH or immediately after.

attrs.title: Different registration systems by species
attrs.body: Cattle movements are recorded in the Cattle Tracing System (CTS), managed by BCMS (British Cattle Movement Service). Sheep and goat movements are recorded in the Sheep and Goat Inventory, managed through EIDCymru or APHA depending on your location. Pig movements use eAML2. All three systems require your CPH as the common identifier, but each has its own portal and process. Register with all relevant species systems before your first movement.
